questions.question: Define a process and explain the difference between a program and a process.
keyPoints: Process is a program in execution
Program is passive, process is active
Multiple processes can run same program
Process has resources like memory, CPU time

questions.question: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List any four information stored in a PCB.
keyPoints: PCB is a data structure containing process information
Stores: Process ID, State, CPU registers
Memory management info, I/O status
Accounting and scheduling information

questions.question: Draw and explain the process state diagram with all five basic states.
keyPoints: Five states: NEW, READY, RUNNING, WAITING, TERMINATED
Transitions: NEW to READY, READY to RUNNING
RUNNING to READY (preempt), RUNNING to WAITING (I/O)
WAITING to READY (I/O complete), RUNNING to TERMINATED

questions.question: Explain the concept of context switching in operating systems.
keyPoints: Context switch saves state of current process
Restores state of another process
Involves saving registers, program counter
Overhead operation, affects system performance

questions.question: Differentiate between zombie process and orphan process.
keyPoints: Zombie: terminated child, parent hasn't collected status
Orphan: running child, parent has terminated
Zombie remains in process table
Orphan is adopted by init process

questions.question: Explain the process creation mechanism in operating systems.
keyPoints: Parent process creates child process using system calls
In Unix: fork() creates new process
Child gets copy of parent's address space
Parent and child execute concurrently after fork()

questions.question: What is a daemon process? Give two examples of daemon processes.
keyPoints: Daemon is a background process with no terminal
Runs continuously providing system services
Examples: systemd/init, httpd (web server)
sshd (secure shell daemon), cron (task scheduler)

questions.question: Explain in detail the life cycle of a process from creation to termination. Include all possible state transitions in your answer.
keyPoints: NEW: Process being created, OS allocates memory and PCB
READY: Admitted to ready queue, waiting for CPU
RUNNING: Currently executing on CPU
WAITING: Blocked for I/O or event
TERMINATED: Completed execution, resources freed
Explain all transitions between states
Role of scheduler and dispatcher
Context switching mechanism

questions.question: Explain the structure and contents of Process Control Block (PCB). How does the OS use PCB for process management?
keyPoints: PCB is kernel data structure
Contains: Process info, State, Registers
Memory management: page tables, segment tables
I/O status: open files, pending I/O
Accounting: CPU time used, limits
Scheduling: priority, queue pointers
OS uses PCB to switch contexts
All process operations reference PCB

questions.question: What is process scheduling? Explain the three levels of process scheduling with their functions.
keyPoints: Scheduling decides which process gets CPU
Long-term (admission): selects processes to admit
Controls degree of multiprogramming
Short-term (CPU): selects ready process for CPU
Medium-term: swaps processes in/out of memory
Dispatcher gives CPU to selected process
Explain context switching overhead

questions.question: Explain the fork() system call used for process creation in Unix. What happens to parent and child processes after fork()?
keyPoints: fork() creates new process (child)
Returns twice: once to parent (child's PID), once to child (0)
Child gets copy of parent's address space
Separate memory spaces after fork()
Parent and child execute concurrently
Parent can wait for child using wait()
exec() can replace child's memory with new program
